I agree with the previous conclusion. The primary purpose of using a 95% confidence interval in this context is indeed to evaluate both potential efficacy and lack thereof by providing a plausible range around the estimate of treatment effects. This approach allows researchers to understand whether any detected differences between the drug group and the placebo could reasonably include zero (meaning there's no real difference), thus capturing both possibilities within its bounds. Given this duality, option C correctly reflects the complete function of the confidence interval in clinical trial assessments.
